Spring 2026" format: revealjs: theme: default highlight-style: github incremental: false fig-width: 9 fig-height: 3.5 scrollable: true slide-number: true execute: echo: true warning: false --- # Parallel Iterations & Looping Variants **Week 5** ## Agenda {.smaller} * Discuss `map2_()*` and `pmap_()*` (parallel iterations) * `walk()` and friends * `modify()` * `safely()` * `reduce()` **Learning objectives** * Understand the differences between `map`, `map2`, and `pmap` * Know when to apply `walk()` instead of `map()`, and why it may be useful * Understand the similarities and differences between `map()` and `modify()` * Diagnose errors with `safely()` and understand other situations where it may be helpful * Collapsing/reducing lists with `purrr::reduce()` or `base::Reduce()` ## Quick Review: `nest_by()` {.smaller} `dplyr::nest_by()` - creates a list column for each group, **AND** - converts it to a `rowwise` data frame Think of it as: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} group_by() |> summarise() ``` ::: ...and storing the remaining columns as a nested tibble ready for `rowwise()` applications We'll come back to this # `map2()` ## `map2()` ![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/7a545699ff7069a98329fcfbe6e42b734507eb16/211a5/diagrams/functionals/map2-arg.png) ## Two examples 1) Basic simulations - iterating over two vectors 2) Plots by month, changing the title ## 1) Iterating over two vectors * Simulate data from a normal distribution + Vary $n$ from 5 to 150 by increments of 5 + For each $n$, vary $\mu$ (mean) from -2 to 2 by increments of 0.25 . . . How do we get all combinations? . . . `expand.grid()` ## `expand.grid()` Bonus: It turns it into a data frame! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ints <- 1:3 lets <- c("a", "b", "c") expand.grid(ints, lets) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Var1 Var2 1 1 a 2 2 a 3 3 a 4 1 b 5 2 b 6 3 b 7 1 c 8 2 c 9 3 c ``` ::: ::: ## Set conditions [**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} conditions <- expand.grid( n = seq(5, 150, 5), mu = seq(-2, 2, 0.25) ) nrow(conditions) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 510 ``` ::: ::: :::: {.columns} More powerful (this is my preferred method) ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} sim2 <- conditions |> as_tibble() |> # Not required, but definitely helpful mutate(sim = map2(n, mu, ~rnorm(n = .x, mean = .y, sd = 10))) sim2 ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 510 × 3 n mu sim 1 5 -2 2 10 -2 3 15 -2 4 20 -2 5 25 -2 6 30 -2 7 35 -2 8 40 -2 9 45 -2 10 50 -2 # ℹ 500 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Unnest ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|4"} conditions |> as_tibble() |> mutate(sim = map2(n, mu, ~rnorm(.x, .y, sd = 10))) |> unnest(sim) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 39,525 × 3 n mu sim 1 5 -2 -8.30 2 5 -2 -8.73 3 5 -2 17.9 4 5 -2 -9.92 5 5 -2 2.16 6 10 -2 -4.57 7 10 -2 1.22 8 10 -2 6.95 9 10 -2 -3.84 10 10 -2 1.11 # ℹ 39,515 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Challenge Can you replicate what we just did, but using a `rowwise()` approach? ``{=html} . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|1|2|3|3"} conditions |> rowwise() |> mutate(sim = list(rnorm(n, mu, sd = 10))) |> unnest(sim) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 39,525 × 3 n mu sim 1 5 -2 0.797 2 5 -2 -11.7 3 5 -2 -10.9 4 5 -2 9.99 5 5 -2 -7.51 6 10 -2 -7.87 7 10 -2 -2.85 8 10 -2 -10.4 9 10 -2 7.53 10 10 -2 -18.6 # ℹ 39,515 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Remember {.smaller} - `mutate()` expects each a vector of the same length as the data frame — one value per row - `rnorm(n, mu, sd = 10)` returns a vector of length `n`, not a single value - Without `list()`, `{dplyr}` would try to unpack those `n` values directly into the column, causing a length mismatch error - Wrapping in `list()` boxes the vector into of length = 1 + a list with one element + so `mutate()` sees exactly one element per row: a length-1 list containing the vector . . . With `list()`: `row 1 → list(c(0.3, -1.2, 0.8)) ` [✓ length 1]{style="color:#009E73;"} `row 2 → list(c(0.1, 2.4, ...))` [✓ length 1]{style="color:#009E73;"} . . . Without `list()`: `row 1 → c(0.3, -1.2, 0.8)` [✗ length 3 — doesn't fit in one row]{style="color:#dd5129;"} ## 2) Plots by month, changing the title [**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"} The data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(fivethirtyeight) pulitzer ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 50 × 7 newspaper circ2004 circ2013 pctchg_circ num_finals1990_2003 1 USA Today 2192098 1674306 -24 1 2 Wall Street Journal 2101017 2378827 13 30 3 New York Times 1119027 1865318 67 55 4 Los Angeles Times 983727 653868 -34 44 5 Washington Post 760034 474767 -38 52 6 New York Daily News 712671 516165 -28 4 7 New York Post 642844 500521 -22 0 8 Chicago Tribune 603315 414930 -31 23 9 San Jose Mercury News 558874 583998 4 4 10 Newsday 553117 377744 -32 12 # ℹ 40 more rows # ℹ 2 more variables: num_finals2004_2014 , num_finals1990_2014 ``` ::: ::: ## Prep data ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} pulitzer <- fivethirtyeight::pulitzer |> select(newspaper, starts_with("num")) |> pivot_longer( -newspaper, names_to = "year_range", values_to = "n", names_prefix = "num_finals" ) |> mutate(year_range = str_replace_all(year_range, "_", "-")) |> filter(year_range != "1990-2014") head(pulitzer) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 3 newspaper year_range n 1 USA Today 1990-2003 1 2 USA Today 2004-2014 1 3 Wall Street Journal 1990-2003 30 4 Wall Street Journal 2004-2014 20 5 New York Times 1990-2003 55 6 New York Times 2004-2014 62 ``` ::: ::: ## One plot {.smaller} ::: {.cell output-location='column-fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} wsj <- pulitzer |> filter(newspaper == "Wall Street Journal") ggplot(wsj, aes(n, year_range)) + geom_col(aes(fill = n)) + scale_x_continuous( limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), expand = c(0, 0) ) + guides(fill = "none") + labs( title = "Pulitzer Prize winners: Wall Street Journal", x = "Total number of winners", y = "" ) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w5_files/figure-revealjs/one-plot-pulitzer-1.png){width=864} ::: ::: ## Nest data ::: {.cell output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code} by_newspaper <- pulitzer |> group_by(newspaper) |> nest() by_newspaper ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 50 × 2 # Groups: newspaper [50] newspaper data 1 USA Today 2 Wall Street Journal 3 New York Times 4 Los Angeles Times 5 Washington Post 6 New York Daily News 7 New York Post 8 Chicago Tribune 9 San Jose Mercury News 10 Newsday # ℹ 40 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Produce all plots You try first! Don't worry about the correct title yet ``{=html} ## Solution {.smaller} . . . Just the plots ::: {.cell output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|3|4|5-16"} by_newspaper |> mutate( plot = map(data, ~.x |> ggplot(aes(n, year_range)) + geom_col(aes(fill = n)) + scale_x_continuous( limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), expand = c(0, 0) ) + guides(fill = "none") + labs( title = "Pulitzer Prize winners", x = "Total number of winners", y = "" ) ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 50 × 3 # Groups: newspaper [50] newspaper data plot 1 USA Today 2 Wall Street Journal 3 New York Times 4 Los Angeles Times 5 Washington Post 6 New York Daily News 7 New York Post 8 Chicago Tribune 9 San Jose Mercury News 10 Newsday # ℹ 40 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Add title {.smaller} . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|5|6|15"} library(glue) p <- by_newspaper |> mutate( plot = map2(data, newspaper, ~.x |> ggplot(aes(n, year_range)) + geom_col(aes(fill = n)) + scale_x_continuous( limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), expand = c(0, 0) ) + guides(fill = "none") + labs( title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {.y}"), # or paste("Pulitzer Prize winners: ", .y) x = "Total number of winners", y = "" ) ) ) ``` ::: ## ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 50 × 3 # Groups: newspaper [50] newspaper data plot 1 USA Today 2 Wall Street Journal 3 New York Times 4 Los Angeles Times 5 Washington Post 6 New York Daily News 7 New York Post 8 Chicago Tribune 9 San Jose Mercury News 10 Newsday # ℹ 40 more rows ``` ::: ::: ## Look at a couple plots {.smaller} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} p$plot[[1]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w5_files/figure-revealjs/p1-1.png){width=864} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} p$plot[[3]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w5_files/figure-revealjs/p1-2.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} p$plot[[2]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w5_files/figure-revealjs/p2-1.png){width=864} ::: ```{.r .cell-code} p$plot[[4]] ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](w5_files/figure-revealjs/p2-2.png){width=864} ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Challenge (You can probably guess where this is going) . . . Can you reproduce the prior plots using a `rowwise()` approach? ``{=html} ## Solution {.smaller} . . . ::: {.cell output-location='column'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|2|4|5-16|13"} pulitzer |> nest_by(newspaper) |> mutate( plot = list( ggplot(data, aes(n, year_range)) + geom_col(aes(fill = n)) + scale_x_continuous( limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), expand = c(0, 0) ) + guides(fill = "none") + labs( title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {newspaper}"), x = "Total number of winners", y = "" ) ) ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 50 × 3 # Rowwise: newspaper newspaper data plot > 1 Arizona Republic [2 × 2] 2 Atlanta Journal Constitution [2 × 2] 3 Baltimore Sun [2 × 2] 4 Boston Globe [2 × 2] 5 Boston Herald [2 × 2] 6 Charlotte Observer [2 × 2] 7 Chicago Sun-Times [2 × 2] 8 Chicago Tribune [2 × 2] 9 Cleveland Plain Dealer [2 × 2] 10 Columbus Dispatch [2 × 2] # ℹ 40 more rows ``` ::: ::: # `pmap()` Iterating over $n$ vectors ## ![](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/diagrams/functionals/pmap-3.png) . . . But we can have as many elements `p` as we want ## Simulation * Simulate data from a normal distribution + Vary $n$ from 5 to 150 by increments of 5 + For each $n$, vary $\mu$ (mean) from -2 to 2 by increments of 0.25 + For each $n$ and $\mu$, vary each $\sigma$ (*SD*) from 1 to 3 by increments of 0.1 ## Simulation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} full_conditions <- expand.grid( n = seq(5, 150, 5), mu = seq(-2, 2, 0.25), sd = seq(1, 3, .1) ) nrow(full_conditions) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 10710 ``` ::: ::: :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r `pmap()`{.smaller} * Parallel iterations greatly increase the things you can do - iterating through at least two things simultaneously is pretty common * The `nest_by()` approach can regularly get you the same result as `group_by() |> nest() |> mutate() |> map()` + Caveat - must be in a data frame, which means working with list columns * Probably still worth learning both + Looping with `{purrr}` is super flexible and often safer than base versions (type safe) + Doesn't have to be used within a data frame # Looping variants ## Other loooping fnctions * `walk()` and friends * `modify()` * `safely()` * `reduce()` ## What we want to know * Know when to apply `walk()` instead of `map()`, and why it may be useful * Understand the parallels and differences between `map()` and `modify()` * Diagnose errors with `safely()` and understand other situations where it may be helpful * Collapsing/reducing lists with `purrr::reduce()` or `base::Reduce()` ## Setup Let's go back to our plotting example: **Saving** * We saw last time that we could use `nest_by()` + Required a bit of awkwardness with adding the paths to the data frame * Instead, we'll do it again but with the `walk()` family ## Why `walk()`? {.smaller} - `walk()` is an alternative to `map` that you use when you want to call a function for its side effects, **NOT** than for its return value - You typically do this because you want to render output to the screen or save files to disk - The important thing is the action, not the return value ::: aside [r4ds](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/iteration.html#walk) ::: ## More practical - If you use `walk()`, nothing will get printed to the screen - This is particularly helpful for quarto files - Most often use it for 1. saving 2. printing ## Example {visibility="hidden"} [**Please do the following**]{style="color:#009E73;"} * Create a new Quarto document * Paste the code you have for creating the Pulitzer plots in a code chunk there + see next slide ## `final_plots` code {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(fivethirtyeight) library(english) library(tidyverse) library(glue) pulitzer <- fivethirtyeight::pulitzer |> select(newspaper, starts_with("num")) |> pivot_longer( -newspaper, names_to = "year_range", values_to = "n", names_prefix = "num_finals" ) |> mutate(year_range = str_replace_all(year_range, "_", "-")) |> filter(year_range != "1990-2014") |> group_by(newspaper) |> mutate(tot = sum(n)) |> mutate( label = glue( "{str_to_title(as.english(tot))} Total Pulitzer Awards" ) ) by_newspaper_label <- pulitzer |> group_by(newspaper, label) |> nest() final_plots <- by_newspaper_label |> mutate(plots = pmap(list(newspaper, label, data), ~ggplot(..3, aes(n, year_range)) + geom_col(aes(fill = n)) + scale_x_continuous( limits = c(0, max(pulitzer$n)), expand = c(0, 0) ) + guides(fill = "none") + labs( title = glue("Pulitzer Prize winners: {..1}"), x = "Total number of winners", y = "", caption = ..2 ) ) ) ``` ::: ## Create a directory {visibility="hidden"} We already did this, but in case we hadn't... ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fs::dir_create(here::here("plots", "pulitzers")) ``` ::: ## Create file paths {visibility="hidden"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} newspapers <- str_replace_all( tolower(final_plots$newspaper), " ", "-" ) paths <- here::here( "plots", "pulitzers", glue("{newspapers}.png") ) ``` ::: ## Challenge {visibility="hidden"} * Use a `map()` family function to loop through `paths` and `final_plots$plots` to save all plots * Render your file. "Bb" [[3]] [1] "Cc" ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} modify2(LETTERS[1:3], letters[1:3], paste0) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Aa" "Bb" "Cc" ``` ::: ::: ## `safely()` Errors during iterations - Sometimes a loop will work for most cases, but return an error on a few - Often, you want to return the output you can - Alternatively, you might want to diagnose *where* the error is occurring . . . `purrr::safely()` ## Example [**Please run this code**]{style="color:#009E73;"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_cyl <- mpg |> group_by(cyl) |> nest() by_cyl ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 2 # Groups: cyl [4] cyl data 1 4 2 6 3 8 4 5 ``` ::: ::: ``{=html} ## Now try to fit a model [**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"} Notice the error message is *super* helpful! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} by_cyl |> mutate(mod = map(data, ~lm(hwy ~ displ + drv, data = .x))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `mutate()`: ℹ In argument: `mod = map(data, ~lm(hwy ~ displ + drv, data = .x))`. ℹ In group 2: `cyl = 5`. Caused by error in `map()`: ℹ In index: 1. Caused by error in `contrasts<-`: ! contrasts can be applied only to factors with 2 or more levels ``` ::: ::: ## Safe return First, define safe function - note that this will work for **ANY** function ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_lm <- safely(lm) ``` ::: . . . Next, loop the safe function, instead of the standard function ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|2"} safe_models <- by_cyl |> mutate(safe_mod = map(data, ~safe_lm(hwy ~ displ + drv, data = .x))) safe_models ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 3 # Groups: cyl [4] cyl data safe_mod 1 4 2 6 3 8 4 5 ``` ::: ::: ## What's returned? {.smaller} ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_models$safe_mod[[1]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $result Call: .f(formula = ..1, data = ..2) Coefficients: (Intercept) displ drvf 37.370 -5.289 3.882 $error NULL ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_models$safe_mod[[4]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $result NULL $error ``` ::: ::: ## Inspecting I often use `safely()` to help me de-bug Why is it failing *there* (but note the error message here helps with this too) . . . Create a new variable to filter for results with errors ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_models |> mutate(error = map_lgl(safe_mod, ~!is.null(.x$error))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 # Groups: cyl [4] cyl data safe_mod error 1 4 FALSE 2 6 FALSE 3 8 FALSE 4 5 TRUE ``` ::: ::: ## Inspecting the data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_models |> mutate(error = map_lgl(safe_mod, ~!is.null(.x$error))) |> filter(error) |> select(cyl, data) |> unnest(data) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 11 # Groups: cyl [1] cyl manufacturer model displ year trans drv cty hwy fl class 1 5 volkswagen jetta 2.5 2008 auto(… f 21 29 r comp… 2 5 volkswagen jetta 2.5 2008 manua… f 21 29 r comp… 3 5 volkswagen new beetle 2.5 2008 manua… f 20 28 r subc… 4 5 volkswagen new beetle 2.5 2008 auto(… f 20 29 r subc… ``` ::: ::: . . . `lm(hwy ~ displ + drv)` The `displ` and `drv` variables are constant, so no relation can be estimated (no variance) ## Pull results that worked ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} safe_models |> mutate(results = map(safe_mod, "result")) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 # Groups: cyl [4] cyl data safe_mod results 1 4 2 6 3 8 4 5 ``` ::: ::: ## Pull results that worked Now we can `broom::tidy()` or similar ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|3|4|5"} safe_models |> mutate(results = map(safe_mod, "result"), tidied = map(results, broom::tidy)) |> select(cyl, tidied) |> unnest(tidied) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 11 × 6 # Groups: cyl [3] cyl term estimate std.error statistic p.value 1 4 (Intercept) 37.4 3.54 10.6 1.05e-16 2 4 displ -5.29 1.44 -3.68 4.24e- 4 3 4 drvf 3.88 0.997 3.89 2.07e- 4 4 6 (Intercept) 28.0 2.35 11.9 5.72e-19 5 6 displ -2.33 0.637 -3.66 4.65e- 4 6 6 drvf 4.57 0.601 7.60 6.79e-11 7 6 drvr 6.38 1.23 5.19 1.71e- 6 8 8 (Intercept) 14.8 2.89 5.13 2.71e- 6 9 8 displ 0.306 0.572 0.535 5.94e- 1 10 8 drvf 8.56 2.68 3.19 2.16e- 3 11 8 drvr 3.71 0.732 5.07 3.47e- 6 ``` ::: ::: ## When else might we use this? How about web scraping - pages change and URLs don't always work ## Example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(rvest) links <- list( "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FC_Barcelona", "https://nosuchpage", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rome" ) pages <- map(links, ~{ Sys.sleep(0.1) read_html(.x) }) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-error} ``` Error in `map()`: ℹ In index: 2. Caused by error in `open.connection()`: ! cannot open the connection ``` ::: ::: ## The problem I can't connect to because it doesn't exist . . . **BUT** . . . That also means I can't get *any* of my links because **one** page errored Imagine it was 1 in 1,000 instead of 1 in 3! ## `safely()` to the rescue Safe version ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="|1|4"} safe_read_html <- safely(read_html) pages <- map(links, ~{ Sys.sleep(0.1) safe_read_html(.x) }) str(pages) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` List of 3 $ :List of 2 ..$ result:List of 2 .. ..$ node: .. ..$ doc : .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "xml_document" "xml_node" ..$ error : NULL $ :List of 2 ..$ result: NULL ..$ error :List of 2 .. ..$ message: chr "cannot open the connection" .. ..$ call : language open.connection(x, "rb") .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:3] "simpleError" "error" "condition" $ :List of 2 ..$ result:List of 2 .. ..$ node: .. ..$ doc : .. ..- attr(*, "class")= chr [1:2] "xml_document" "xml_node" ..$ error : NULL ``` ::: ::: ## Non-results In a real example, we'd probably want to double-check the pages where we got no results ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} errors <- map_lgl(pages, ~!is.null(.x$error)) links[errors] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] "https://nosuchpage" ``` ::: ::: ## `reduce()` Reducing a list - The `map()` family of functions will always return a vector the same length as the input - `reduce()` will collapse or reduce the list to a single element ## Example ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} l <- list( c(1, 3), c(1, 5, 7, 9), 3, c(4, 8, 12, 2) ) ``` ::: . . . :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} `map()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} map(l, sum) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] [1] 4 [[2]] [1] 22 [[3]] [1] 3 [[4]] [1] 26 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%" .fragment} `reduce()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reduce(l, sum) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 55 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## What's going on? The code `reduce(l, sum)` is the same as ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sum(l[[4]], sum(l[[3]], sum(l[[1]], l[[2]]))) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 55 ``` ::: ::: Or slightly differently ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} first_sum <- sum(l[[1]], l[[2]]) second_sum <- sum(first_sum, l[[3]]) final_sum <- sum(second_sum, l[[4]]) final_sum ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 55 ``` ::: ::: ## Why might you use this? {.smaller} What if you had a list of data frames like this ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} (l_df <- list( tibble(id = 1:3, score = rnorm(3)), tibble(id = 1:5, treatment = rbinom(5, 1, .5)), tibble(id = c(1, 3, 5, 7), other_thing = rnorm(4)) )) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] # A tibble: 3 × 2 id score 1 1 0.668 2 2 -0.386 3 3 1.62 [[2]] # A tibble: 5 × 2 id treatment 1 1 0 2 2 0 3 3 0 4 4 1 5 5 0 [[3]] # A tibble: 4 × 2 id other_thing 1 1 -0.218 2 3 0.295 3 5 1.59 4 7 0.368 ``` ::: ::: ## Why might you use this? We can join these all together with a single loop ::: {.cell output-location='fragment'} ```{.r .cell-code} reduce(l_df, full_join) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 4 id score treatment other_thing 1 1 0.668 0 -0.218 2 2 -0.386 0 NA 3 3 1.62 0 0.295 4 4 NA 1 NA 5 5 NA 0 1.59 6 7 NA NA 0.368 ``` ::: ::: ## Caution You have to be careful on directionality :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} `left_join()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reduce(l_df, left_join) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 3 × 4 id score treatment other_thing 1 1 0.668 0 -0.218 2 2 -0.386 0 NA 3 3 1.62 0 0.295 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} `right_join()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reduce(l_df, right_join) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 id score treatment other_thing 1 1 0.668 0 -0.218 2 3 1.62 0 0.295 3 5 NA 0 1.59 4 7 NA NA 0.368 ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Another example {.smaller} If you have the same columns, you probably just want to `bind_rows()` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} (l_df2 <- list( tibble(id = 1:3, scid = 1, score = rnorm(3)), tibble(id = 1:5, scid = 2, score = rnorm(5)), tibble(id = c(1, 3, 5, 7), scid = 3, score = rnorm(4)) )) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [[1]] # A tibble: 3 × 3 id scid score 1 1 1 0.00148 2 2 1 -0.231 3 3 1 1.15 [[2]] # A tibble: 5 × 3 id scid score 1 1 2 -0.982 2 2 2 -0.194 3 3 2 -1.15 4 4 2 1.79 5 5 2 -1.45 [[3]] # A tibble: 4 × 3 id scid score 1 1 3 0.0523 2 3 3 1.01 3 5 3 0.0133 4 7 3 -1.98 ``` ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} reduce(l_df2, bind_rows) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 12 × 3 id scid score 1 1 1 0.00148 2 2 1 -0.231 3 3 1 1.15 4 1 2 -0.982 5 2 2 -0.194 6 3 2 -1.15 7 4 2 1.79 8 5 2 -1.45 9 1 3 0.0523 10 3 3 1.01 11 5 3 0.0133 12 7 3 -1.98 ``` ::: ::: ## Non-loop version Luckily, the prior slide has become obsolete, because `bind_rows()` will do the list reduction for us ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bind_rows(l_df2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 12 × 3 id scid score 1 1 1 0.00148 2 2 1 -0.231 3 3 1 1.15 4 1 2 -0.982 5 2 2 -0.194 6 3 2 -1.15 7 4 2 1.79 8 5 2 -1.45 9 1 3 0.0523 10 3 3 1.01 11 5 3 0.0133 12 7 3 -1.98 ``` ::: ::: ## Another example This is a poor example, but there are use cases like this ::: {.cell} <script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
